statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
There is a graph of $ n^2 $ vertices, where vertices are labeled by integer pairs $ (r,c) $ such that $ 1 \le r,c \le n $ . Vertices $ (r_1,c_1) $ and $ (r_2,c_2) $ are directly connected if and only if $ (r_1-r_2)^2+(c_1-c_2)^2=\color{red}{13} $ . This graph is called the Zebra Graph of dimensions $ n \times n $ .

Please find a subset of vertices $ S $ in the Zebra Graph of dimensions $ n \times n $ , which satisfies the following condition.

- The graph induced $ ^{\text{∗}} $ by the subset $ S $ is isomorphic to a cycle graph of at least $ \left\lfloor{\frac{n^2}{e}}\right\rfloor $ vertices $ ^{\text{†}} $ .

It can be shown that such a subset of vertices exists under the constraints of this problem.

$ ^{\text{∗}} $ The induced graph of a subset of vertices $ X $ is a graph that contains all vertices in $ X $ and all edges whose both endpoints are in $ X $ .

$ ^{\text{†}} $ Here, $ e $ is the mathematical constant equal to the limit $ \lim \limits_{n \to \infty} \left ({1 + \frac{1}{n}} \right )^n \approx 2.71828182 $ . Note that the value of $ \frac{1}{e} $ is approximately $ 0.36787944 $ .

Input Format
The first and only line of input contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ n\in \{5,1001\} $ ).

There are only two input files for this problem:

- The first input file (the example input) has $ n=5 $ ;
- The second input file has $ n=1001 $ .

Hacks are disabled for this problem.

Output Format
Output $ n $ lines, each containing a string $ s_i $ of length $ n $ denoting the $ i $ -th row of the graph. If the vertex $ (r,c) $ is an element of $ S $ , then the $ c $ -th letter of $ s_r $ should be '1'. Otherwise, the $ c $ -th letter of $ s_r $ should be '0'.

If there are multiple solutions, print any of them.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
Output
01110
11011
10001
11011
01110

Note
For the example output, the induced graph corresponding to the subset $ S $ is shown below.

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2201G/a4314cb7035c0308055fb010a1142145ee83133033b4192be6493fe655e8e8c2.png)This graph is isomorphic to the cycle graph $ C_{16} $ consisting of $ 16 $ vertices. As $ 16 \ge \left\lfloor{\frac{n^2}{e}}\right\rfloor = 9 $ , the output satisfies the problem's condition.

hints.title: Hints (or as it turned out closer to, a guide?)
hints.content:
2201G - Codeforces Heuristic Contest 1001
One quick disclaimer before getting into what we're actually trying to do in the solution: the value $$$1/e$$$ didn't really have anything to do with the real solution. That's just for a little fun factor in the problem; the actual model solution aims for a density of $$$0.4 - o(1)$$$. It uses $$$385\,160$$$ vertices, where at least $$$368\,615$$$ vertices are required to get accepted.
The main concept that you would need to understand in order to solve this problem would be
"gadgets"
. In Wikipedia, it is defined as "subunit of a problem instance that simulates the behavior of one of the fundamental units of a different computational problem". You don't have to be scared about terminology right now, though; understanding "subunit of a problem" "simulating units of another computational problem" is enough for this problem.
Let's look at the following example:
We can call that a "gadget" that simulates an edge in a path graph, considering that it can be extended arbitrarily and looks like an edge (well, close enough)..... though we don't really have something that we would call a vertex. So what we're trying to do in a nutshell is to find a pattern that repeats well without making violations with other patterns. And it would be great if they can interconnect with some other patterns as well.
One nice pattern made of gadgets, though it doesn't get AC on the problem, would be the following:
This pattern doesn't make any inner cycles, repeats well enough, and is made of easily extensible gadgets. That's why gadgets are important in solving this problem; they make up the framework of the main pattern in any construction.
Now, constructing a good gadget can be done in two ways in general:
Draw on paper until you get a good one. This is not impossible, but it's hard in general. Definitely not impossible though, sammyuri did it in this way!
Define the "rules" that the gadget must satisfy, and optimize that using heuristics or optimization libraries. Trying out some solvers such as PuLP (free) or Gurobi (proprietary) would be a good start. It's still not easy, but at least it must be less manual labor, right?
The advantage of the second method is that those algorithms and libraries will optimize
for
you according to the rules you set; so your role changes from laying out the pattern to planning how the pattern should behave.
Now the final objective of the problem is to find good gadgets and weave a pattern that achieves at least $$$0.4-o(1)$$$ density. $$$0.4-o(1)$$$ means that it converges to $$$0.4$$$ from below, so small amounts of empty areas can be wasted without much concern; in the end, you will see the forest, not the trees. How will you do that? That's up to you. Good luck, and have fun!
